text: This video will demonstrate an en bloc minimally invasive DII resection for proximal gastric cancer in a North American context. This patient is a seventy-three year old male who presented with anemia. A large ulcerated lesion was identified on endoscopy in the gastric cardia and was biopsy proven to be an adenocarcinoma. Saging CT did not show any evidence of local invasion of metastatic disease.

The tumor is visible on the anterior stomach. The surgeon uses electrocautery and ligature to separate the greater omentum and dissect into the lesser sac, confirming no posterior extension of the tumor into the pancreas, mesocolon, or major vessels.

The surgeon identifies and ligates the right gastroepiploic artery using a ligature. The distal stomach is dissected off the pancreas, followed by the dissection of portal lymph nodes. The peritoneum is divided to expose the common bile duct, portal vein, and common hepatic artery. The right gastric artery is identified, isolated, and ligated, ensuring no involvement of portal structures. The surgeon clears hepatoduodenal nodes medial to the portal structures and divides the gastrohepatic ligament.

The surgeon mobilizes the remainder of the stomach from the pancreas and isolates the left gastric vessels. The left gastric vein is ligated with a ligature, and the left gastric artery is ligated using clips and a ligature. The distal stomach and proximal duodenum are fully mobilized, and the duodenum is divided distal to the pylorus using a 60mm tan tri-stapler. The portal dissection results and removal of hepatoduodenal nodes are visible.

The surgeon identifies the common bile duct, right and left hepatic arteries, celiac axis, and the origin of the splenic artery. The splenic artery nodes are cleared off the pancreas. The stomach is dissected away from the spleen, with the splenic hilum nodes cleared and the short gastric vessels divided using a ligature.

The surgeon dissects the pericardial nodes while mobilizing the proximal stomach and esophagus. The peritoneum over the esophagus is divided circumferentially using cautery, allowing the esophagus to be retracted inferiorly. The esophagus is then divided with a sixty millimeter purple linear tri-stapler. The completely mobilized specimen is placed above the liver to facilitate reconstruction.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately fifty centimeters of small bowel, which is divided using a forty-five millimeter white linear stapler. A side-to-side jejunojejunostomy is created with a sixty millimeter white linear stapler, and the enteric defect is closed with a three-year absorbable suture. For the esophagojejunal anastomosis, a circular EEA stapler is used; the anvil is passed through the mouth, and the stapler is inserted through a small incision in the abdominal wall, protected by a wound protector. The stapler is inserted through the end of the Roux limb, deploying the spike through the anti-mesenteric wall of the bowel.

The surgeon staples the end of the jejunum closed using a sixty millimeter white linear stapler. The anastomosis is reinforced with a hand-sewn layer of absorbable suture circumferentially. A nasogastric tube is passed through the anastomosis, and air and blue dye are insufflated to test the integrity of the anastomosis. The specimen is removed through a small fan and seal incision.
